; t01-hello.spc — Hello World end-to-end test
; Expected output: Hello\n

.data msg 72, 101, 108, 108, 111, 10, 0

.proc main 0
    push msg
    call puts
    halt
.end

.proc puts 1
    loada 0
    storel 0
loop:
    loadl 0
    loadb
    dup
    jz done
    sys 1
    loadl 0
    push 1
    add
    storel 0
    jmp loop
done:
    drop
    ret 1
.end

10	    .word 0     ; esp  — eval stack pointer (top of eval stack)
11	    .word 0     ; csp  — call stack pointer (top of call stack)
12	    .word 0     ; fp   — current frame pointer (call stack)
13	    .word 0     ; gp   — globals base address
14	    .word 0     ; hp   — heap pointer (next free)
15	    .word 0     ; code — base address of p-code segment
16	    .word 0     ; status — 0=running, 1=halted, 2=trapped

27	| `push_s` imm8 | 2 bytes | ( -- n ) | Push sign-extended 8-bit immediate |
28	| `dup` | 1 byte | ( a -- a a ) | Duplicate top |
29	| `drop` | 1 byte | ( a -- ) | Discard top |
30	| `swap` | 1 byte | ( a b -- b a ) | Swap top two |
31	| `over` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a b a ) | Copy second to top |
32

38	| `sub` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a-b ) | Subtract |
39	| `mul` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a*b ) | Multiply |
40	| `div` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a/b ) | Divide (trap on b=0) |
41	| `mod` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a%b ) | Modulo (trap on b=0) |
42	| `neg` | 1 byte | ( a -- -a ) | Negate |

44	| `or` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a\|b ) | Bitwise OR |
45	| `xor` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a^b ) | Bitwise XOR |
46	| `not` | 1 byte | ( a -- ~a ) | Bitwise NOT |
47	| `shl` | 1 byte | ( a n -- a<<n ) | Shift left |
48	| `shr` | 1 byte | ( a n -- a>>n ) | Arithmetic shift right |

70	| `call` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( args... -- ) | Call procedure (static link = 0) |
71	| `calln` depth8 addr24 | 5 bytes | ( args... -- ) | Call with static link (depth=0: nested, depth=N: N levels up) |

file.content:
# pv24a — Design Document

## 1. VM State

The p-code VM maintains the following state in a memory struct:

```
vm_state:
    .word 0     ; pc   — p-code instruction pointer
    .word 0     ; esp  — eval stack pointer (top of eval stack)
    .word 0     ; csp  — call stack pointer (top of call stack)
    .word 0     ; fp   — current frame pointer (call stack)
    .word 0     ; gp   — globals base address
    .word 0     ; hp   — heap pointer (next free)
    .word 0     ; code — base address of p-code segment
    .word 0     ; status — 0=running, 1=halted, 2=trapped
    .word 0     ; trap_code — last trap reason
```

## 2. P-Code Instruction Set

### 2.1 Stack / Constants

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `push` imm24 | 4 bytes | ( -- n ) | Push 24-bit immediate |
| `push_s` imm8 | 2 bytes | ( -- n ) | Push sign-extended 8-bit immediate |
| `dup` | 1 byte | ( a -- a a ) | Duplicate top |
| `drop` | 1 byte | ( a -- ) | Discard top |
| `swap` | 1 byte | ( a b -- b a ) | Swap top two |
| `over` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a b a ) | Copy second to top |

### 2.2 Arithmetic / Logic

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `add` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a+b ) | Add |
| `sub` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a-b ) | Subtract |
| `mul` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a*b ) | Multiply |
| `div` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a/b ) | Divide (trap on b=0) |
| `mod` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a%b ) | Modulo (trap on b=0) |
| `neg` | 1 byte | ( a -- -a ) | Negate |
| `and` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a&b ) | Bitwise AND |
| `or` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a\|b ) | Bitwise OR |
| `xor` | 1 byte | ( a b -- a^b ) | Bitwise XOR |
| `not` | 1 byte | ( a -- ~a ) | Bitwise NOT |
| `shl` | 1 byte | ( a n -- a<<n ) | Shift left |
| `shr` | 1 byte | ( a n -- a>>n ) | Arithmetic shift right |

### 2.3 Comparison

All comparisons push 1 (true) or 0 (false):

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `eq` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Equal |
| `ne` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Not equal |
| `lt` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Less than (signed) |
| `le` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Less or equal (signed) |
| `gt` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Greater than (signed) |
| `ge` | 1 byte | ( a b -- flag ) | Greater or equal (signed) |

### 2.4 Control Flow

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `jmp` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( -- ) | Unconditional jump |
| `jz` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( flag -- ) | Jump if zero |
| `jnz` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( flag -- ) | Jump if nonzero |
| `call` addr24 | 4 bytes | ( args... -- ) | Call procedure (static link = 0) |
| `calln` depth8 addr24 | 5 bytes | ( args... -- ) | Call with static link (depth=0: nested, depth=N: N levels up) |
| `ret` nargs8 | 2 bytes | ( [retval] -- [retval] ) | Return, clean nargs |
| `halt` | 1 byte | ( -- ) | Stop VM |
| `trap` code8 | 2 bytes | ( -- ) | Trigger trap |

### 2.5 Local / Global / Nonlocal Access

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `enter` nlocals8 | 2 bytes | ( -- ) | Set up frame, reserve N local slots |
| `leave` | 1 byte | ( -- ) | Tear down frame |
| `loadl` off8 | 2 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load local at offset |
| `storel` off8 | 2 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store local at offset |
| `loadg` off24 | 4 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load global at offset |
| `storeg` off24 | 4 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store global at offset |
| `addrl` off8 | 2 bytes | ( -- addr ) | Push address of local |
| `addrg` off24 | 4 bytes | ( -- addr ) | Push address of global |
| `loadn` depth8 off8 | 3 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load nonlocal via static link chain |
| `storen` depth8 off8 | 3 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store nonlocal via static link chain |

### 2.6 Indirect Memory Access

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `load` | 1 byte | ( addr -- val ) | Load word from address |
| `store` | 1 byte | ( val addr -- ) | Store word to address |
| `loadb` | 1 byte | ( addr -- byte ) | Load byte (zero-extended) |
| `storeb` | 1 byte | ( byte addr -- ) | Store byte |

### 2.7 System / I/O

content.content:
100	### 2.7 System / I/O
101	
102	| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
103	|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
104	| `sys` id8 | 2 bytes | (varies) | System call |
105	
106	System call IDs:
107	
108	| ID | Name | Stack Effect | Description |
109	|----|------|-------------|-------------|
110	| 0 | HALT | ( -- ) | Stop execution |
111	| 1 | PUTC | ( char -- ) | Write byte to UART |
112	| 2 | GETC | ( -- char ) | Read byte from UART (blocking) |
113	| 3 | LED | ( state -- ) | Write LED state |
114	| 4 | ALLOC | ( size -- ptr ) | Bump-allocate heap block |
115	| 5 | FREE | ( ptr -- ) | Free heap block (no-op in bump mode) |
116	
117	### 2.8 Argument Access
118	
119	| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
120	|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
121	| `loada` idx8 | 2 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load argument by index |
122	| `storea` idx8 | 2 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store argument by index |
123	
124	## 3. Call Frame Layout
125	
126	When `call` executes, a new frame is pushed onto the call stack:
127	
128	```
129	         ┌──────────────────┐  ← new csp (after enter)
130	         │ local[N-1]       │  offset = +(N-1)*3 from fp
131	         │ ...              │
132	         │ local[0]         │  offset = 0 from fp
133	fp ───→  ├──────────────────┤
134	         │ procedure ID     │  offset = -3
135	         │ static link      │  offset = -6  (lexically enclosing frame)
136	         │ dynamic link     │  offset = -9  (caller's fp)
137	         │ return PC        │  offset = -12
138	         ├──────────────────┤
139	         │ arg[N-1]         │  offset = -15  (from fp)
140	         │ ...              │
141	         │ arg[0]           │  offset = -15 - (N-1)*3
142	         └──────────────────┘
143	```
144	
145	- **Caller** pushes arguments onto eval stack, then `call addr`
146	- **call** instruction: pops args from eval stack into call stack frame header,
147	  pushes return PC, dynamic link, static link, proc ID
148	- **enter N**: advances csp by N*3 to reserve local slots, sets fp
149	- **leave**: restores fp to dynamic link, restores csp

file.content:
### 2.7 System / I/O

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `sys` id8 | 2 bytes | (varies) | System call |

System call IDs:

| ID | Name | Stack Effect | Description |
|----|------|-------------|-------------|
| 0 | HALT | ( -- ) | Stop execution |
| 1 | PUTC | ( char -- ) | Write byte to UART |
| 2 | GETC | ( -- char ) | Read byte from UART (blocking) |
| 3 | LED | ( state -- ) | Write LED state |
| 4 | ALLOC | ( size -- ptr ) | Bump-allocate heap block |
| 5 | FREE | ( ptr -- ) | Free heap block (no-op in bump mode) |

### 2.8 Argument Access

| Opcode | Encoding | Stack Effect | Description |
|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|
| `loada` idx8 | 2 bytes | ( -- val ) | Load argument by index |
| `storea` idx8 | 2 bytes | ( val -- ) | Store argument by index |

## 3. Call Frame Layout

When `call` executes, a new frame is pushed onto the call stack:

```
         ┌──────────────────┐  ← new csp (after enter)
         │ local[N-1]       │  offset = +(N-1)*3 from fp
         │ ...              │
         │ local[0]         │  offset = 0 from fp
fp ───→  ├──────────────────┤
         │ procedure ID     │  offset = -3
         │ static link      │  offset = -6  (lexically enclosing frame)
         │ dynamic link     │  offset = -9  (caller's fp)
         │ return PC        │  offset = -12
         ├──────────────────┤
         │ arg[N-1]         │  offset = -15  (from fp)
         │ ...              │
         │ arg[0]           │  offset = -15 - (N-1)*3
         └──────────────────┘
```

- **Caller** pushes arguments onto eval stack, then `call addr`
- **call** instruction: pops args from eval stack into call stack frame header,
  pushes return PC, dynamic link, static link, proc ID
- **enter N**: advances csp by N*3 to reserve local slots, sets fp
- **leave**: restores fp to dynamic link, restores csp

content.content:
1	; pvmasm.s — P-Code Assembler + VM for COR24
2	;
3	; Integrated assembler and virtual machine:
4	;   1. Read .spc source from UART
5	;   2. Two-pass assembly to bytecode (code+data contiguous in code_buf)
6	;   3. Execute assembled bytecode in the p-code VM
7	;
8	; Assembly phases:
9	;   Pass 1: collect symbols (labels, procs, consts, globals, data), compute sizes
10	;   Pass 2: emit bytecode, resolve symbol references
11	;
12	; Token types:
13	;   0 = TOK_EOF     end of input
14	;   1 = TOK_NL      newline
15	;   2 = TOK_NUM     integer literal (value in tok_value)
16	;   3 = TOK_IDENT   identifier/mnemonic/name (string in tok_buf)
17	;   4 = TOK_DIR     directive without '.' (string in tok_buf)
18	;   5 = TOK_LABEL   label definition without ':' (string in tok_buf)
19	;   6 = TOK_COMMA   comma separator
20	;
21	; Symbol types:
22	;   0 = SYM_CONST   named constant (value = literal)
23	;   1 = SYM_LABEL   code label (value = code offset)
24	;   2 = SYM_PROC    procedure entry (value = code offset)
25	;   3 = SYM_GLOBAL  global variable (value = global segment offset)
26	;   4 = SYM_DATA    data block (value = data segment offset)
27	;
28	; Operand types (mnemonic table):
29	;   0 = NONE    1-byte instruction
30	;   1 = IMM8    2-byte: opcode + byte
31	;   2 = IMM24   4-byte: opcode + word
32	;   3 = D8_A24  5-byte: opcode + byte + word (calln)
33	;   4 = D8_O8   3-byte: opcode + byte + byte (loadn/storen)
34	;
35	; Register allocation:
36	;   r0 = work/scratch, parameter, return value
37	;   r1 = return address (jal) or scratch
38	;   r2 = scratch, function address for jal
39	;   fp = memory base for indexed loads/stores
40	;   sp = COR24 hardware stack (EBR)
41	;
42	; UART: data at -65280 (0xFF0100), status at -65279 (0xFF0101)
43	;   TX busy = status bit 7 (sign bit via lb sign-extend)
44	;   RX ready = status bit 0
45	
46	; ============================================================
47	; Entry point
48	; ============================================================
49	_start:
50	    ; Print boot message
51	    la r0, msg_boot
52	    la r2, uart_puts
53	    jal r1, (r2)
54	
55	    ; Read all UART input into input_buf
56	    la r2, read_all_input
57	    jal r1, (r2)
58	
59	    ; ---- Pass 1: collect symbols, compute sizes ----
60	    lc r0, 0
61	    la r2, input_pos
62	    sw r0, 0(r2)
63	    ; Prime first character
64	    la r2, lex_advance
65	    jal r1, (r2)
66	    ; Set pass number = 1
67	    lc r0, 1
68	    la r2, pass_num
69	    sb r0, 0(r2)
70	    ; Reset counters
71	    lc r0, 0
72	    la r2, code_addr
73	    sw r0, 0(r2)
74	    la r2, global_offset
75	    sw r0, 0(r2)
76	    la r2, data_offset
77	    sw r0, 0(r2)
78	    la r2, sym_count
79	    sw r0, 0(r2)
80	    ; Initialize name_pool_ptr

; pvmasm.s — P-Code Assembler + VM for COR24
;
; Integrated assembler and virtual machine:
;   1. Read .spc source from UART
;   2. Two-pass assembly to bytecode (code+data contiguous in code_buf)
;   3. Execute assembled bytecode in the p-code VM
;
; Assembly phases:
;   Pass 1: collect symbols (labels, procs, consts, globals, data), compute sizes
;   Pass 2: emit bytecode, resolve symbol references
;
; Token types:
;   0 = TOK_EOF     end of input
;   1 = TOK_NL      newline
;   2 = TOK_NUM     integer literal (value in tok_value)
;   3 = TOK_IDENT   identifier/mnemonic/name (string in tok_buf)
;   4 = TOK_DIR     directive without '.' (string in tok_buf)
;   5 = TOK_LABEL   label definition without ':' (string in tok_buf)
;   6 = TOK_COMMA   comma separator
;
; Symbol types:
;   0 = SYM_CONST   named constant (value = literal)
;   1 = SYM_LABEL   code label (value = code offset)
;   2 = SYM_PROC    procedure entry (value = code offset)
;   3 = SYM_GLOBAL  global variable (value = global segment offset)
;   4 = SYM_DATA    data block (value = data segment offset)
;
; Operand types (mnemonic table):
;   0 = NONE    1-byte instruction
;   1 = IMM8    2-byte: opcode + byte
;   2 = IMM24   4-byte: opcode + word
;   3 = D8_A24  5-byte: opcode + byte + word (calln)
;   4 = D8_O8   3-byte: opcode + byte + byte (loadn/storen)
;
; Register allocation:
;   r0 = work/scratch, parameter, return value
;   r1 = return address (jal) or scratch
;   r2 = scratch, function address for jal
;   fp = memory base for indexed loads/stores
;   sp = COR24 hardware stack (EBR)
;
; UART: data at -65280 (0xFF0100), status at -65279 (0xFF0101)
;   TX busy = status bit 7 (sign bit via lb sign-extend)
;   RX ready = status bit 0

; ============================================================
; Entry point
; ============================================================
_start:
    ; Print boot message
    la r0, msg_boot
    la r2, uart_puts
    jal r1, (r2)

    ; Read all UART input into input_buf
    la r2, read_all_input
    jal r1, (r2)

    ; ---- Pass 1: collect symbols, compute sizes ----
    lc r0, 0
    la r2, input_pos
    sw r0, 0(r2)
    ; Prime first character
    la r2, lex_advance
    jal r1, (r2)
    ; Set pass number = 1
    lc r0, 1
    la r2, pass_num
    sb r0, 0(r2)
    ; Reset counters
    lc r0, 0
    la r2, code_addr
    sw r0, 0(r2)
    la r2, global_offset
    sw r0, 0(r2)
    la r2, data_offset
    sw r0, 0(r2)
    la r2, sym_count
    sw r0, 0(r2)
    ; Initialize name_pool_ptr

Perfect! Now I have enough information to provide a comprehensive answer. Let me compile my findings:

## Summary: Understanding PR24P Linked Programs

Based on my thorough search of the codebase, I can now explain what a "linked program" looks like in the pr24p ecosystem.

### Key Components

A **linked program** is created by combining:

1. **pr24p Runtime Library** (`/Users/mike/github/softwarewrighter/pr24p/src/runtime.spc`)
   - 29+ procedures (runtime stubs) written in p-code assembler
   - Implements Pascal standard library: `_p24p_write_int`, `_p24p_write_bool`, `_p24p_write_ln`, `_p24p_write_str`, heap management, I/O, and type checking functions
   - Wrapped in `.module runtime` / `.export` metadata block / `.endmodule`

2. **User Application Code** (generated by p24p Pascal compiler)
   - Application procedures including `main`
   - Wrapped in `.module app` / `.export main` / `.extern` declarations / `.endmodule`
   - Declares external dependencies (which runtime procedures it calls)

3. **Linker Output** (produced by pl24r)
   - Single merged `.spc` file containing all procedures
   - **Critical ordering:** `main` procedure appears FIRST (VM execution starts at code offset 0)
   - Runtime procedures follow after main
   - All metadata directives (`.module`, `.export`, `.extern`, `.endmodule`) are stripped
   - Only standard `.spc` directives remain: `.proc`, `.end`, `.global`, `.data`, `.const`, labels, instructions

### The Merged Structure

From **`/Users/mike/github/softwarewrighter/pl24r/tests/fixtures/`**, here's the concrete pattern:

**Input: e2e_runtime.spc** (~93 lines)
- Starts with `.module runtime` and `.export` directives
- Contains 3 runtime procedures: `_p24p_write_int`, `_p24p_write_bool`, `_p24p_write_ln`
- Each procedure uses `sys 1` (PUTC) syscalls
- Ends with `.endmodule`

**Input: e2e_app.spc** (~30 lines)
- Starts with `.module app` and `.export main` + `.extern` declarations
- Contains only `main` procedure (calls 3 runtime functions)
- Ends with `.endmodule`

**Output: merged .spc** (after pl24r linking)
- Comments showing module separators: `; --- module: runtime ---` / `; --- module: app ---`
- `.proc main 0` appears FIRST (no metadata)
- Then `.proc _p24p_write_int 1`, `.proc _p24p_write_bool 1`, `.proc _p24p_write_ln 0`
- All `.module`, `.export`, `.extern`, `.endmodule` directives removed
- File is immediately executable by pasm or pa24r assemblers

### Full PR24P Runtime Procedures (29 routines)

**Phase 0 (I/O):** `_p24p_write_int`, `_p24p_write_bool`, `_p24p_write_ln`, `_p24p_write_str`

**Phase 1 (Stdlib):** `_p24p_abs`, `_p24p_odd`, `_p24p_ord`, `_p24p_chr`, `_p24p_succ`, `_p24p_pred`, `_p24p_sqr`, `_p24p_bounds_check`, `_p24p_nil_check`, `_p24p_read_int`, `_p24p_read_char`, `_p24p_read_ln`

**Phase 2 (Heap/Formatting):** `_p24p_heap_init`, `_p24p_new`, `_p24p_dispose`, `_p24p_leak_report`, `_p24p_write_char`, `_p24p_write_int_w`, `_p24p_write_char_w`, `_p24p_write_bool_w`, `_p24p_write_str_w`, `_p24p_eof`, `_p24p_eoln`, `_p24p_subrange_check`

### Real Example Structure

From **`/Users/mike/github/softwarewrighter/pr24p/src/runtime.spc`**, each runtime procedure looks like:

```
.proc _p24p_write_int 1
    loada 0              ; load argument n
    dup                  ; n n
    push 0               ; n n 0
    lt                   ; n (n<0?)
    jz positive
    push 45              ; '-'
    sys 1                ; PUTC
    neg                  ; -n
positive:
    storel 0             ; local0 = abs(n)
    push 0               ; push sentinel
extract:
    loadl 0              ; load n
    push 10
    mod                  ; n % 10
    push 48
    add                  ; n % 10 + '0' = digit char
    ... (more p-code) ...
    ret 1
.end
```

### Integration in Toolchain

**Full Pipeline:**
```
Pascal source (.pas)
  ↓ (p24p compiler on COR24)
.spc files (with .module/.export/.extern metadata)
  ↓ (pl24r linker on host)
merged .spc (main first, runtime follows, no metadata)
  ↓ (pa24r or pasm assembler)
.p24 binary bytecode
  ↓ (loader)
pv24a VM execution
```

### Key Files
- Runtime library: `/Users/mike/github/softwarewrighter/pr24p/src/runtime.spc` (550+ lines)
- Linker: `/Users/mike/github/softwarewrighter/pl24r/` (handles merging & metadata stripping)
- Design docs: `/Users/mike/github/sw-vibe-coding/wiki-rs/work/agent-wiki/PR24P.md`, `PL24R.md`, `P24P.md`
- Test fixtures: `/Users/mike/github/softwarewrighter/pl24r/tests/fixtures/e2e_*.spc`

A linked program is essentially a flat assembly-like image where runtime procedures are prepended/merged with user code, all procedures share a global symbol namespace, and execution begins at `main`.

.module runtime
.export _p24p_write_int
.export _p24p_write_bool
.export _p24p_write_ln

; pr24p — Pascal Runtime Library
; Phase 0: Hand-written .spc stubs for p-code VM syscall wrappers

; _p24p_write_int ( n -- )
; Print signed integer to UART as decimal.
.proc _p24p_write_int 1
    enter 1
    loada 0              ; load argument n
    ; check for negative
    dup                  ; n n
    push 0               ; n n 0
    lt                   ; n (n<0?)
    jz positive
    ; print minus sign
    push 45              ; '-'
    sys 1                ; n
    neg                  ; -n
positive:
    storel 0             ; local0 = abs(n)
    push 0               ; push sentinel
extract:
    loadl 0              ; load n
    push 10
    mod                  ; n % 10
    push 48
    add                  ; n % 10 + '0' = digit char
    loadl 0
    push 10
    div                  ; n / 10
    storel 0             ; update n = n / 10
    loadl 0
    jnz extract          ; if n != 0, extract more digits
print:
    dup
    jz done
    sys 1                ; PUTC digit
    jmp print
done:
    drop                 ; discard sentinel
    leave
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_write_bool ( b -- )
; Print TRUE or FALSE to UART.
.proc _p24p_write_bool 1
    enter 0
    loada 0              ; load argument b
    jz print_false
    ; print "TRUE"
    push 84              ; 'T'
    sys 1
    push 82              ; 'R'
    sys 1
    push 85              ; 'U'
    sys 1
    push 69              ; 'E'
    sys 1
    jmp bool_done
print_false:
    ; print "FALSE"
    push 70              ; 'F'
    sys 1
    push 65              ; 'A'
    sys 1
    push 76              ; 'L'
    sys 1
    push 83              ; 'S'
    sys 1
    push 69              ; 'E'
    sys 1
bool_done:
    leave
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_write_ln ( -- )
; Print newline (LF) to UART via sys 1 (PUTC).
.proc _p24p_write_ln 0
    enter 0
    push 10              ; LF
    sys 1
    leave
    ret 0
.end

.endmodule

.module app
.export main
.extern _p24p_write_int
.extern _p24p_write_bool
.extern _p24p_write_ln

; Simple Pascal-like app: writeln(42); writeln(true); writeln(-7)
; Expected output:
;   42
;   TRUE
;   -7

.proc main 0
    ; writeln(42)
    push 42
    call _p24p_write_int
    call _p24p_write_ln
    ; writeln(true)
    push 1
    call _p24p_write_bool
    call _p24p_write_ln
    ; writeln(-7)
    push -7
    call _p24p_write_int
    call _p24p_write_ln
    halt
.end

.endmodule

.module runtime
.export _p24p_write_int
.export _p24p_write_bool

; pr24p — Pascal Runtime Library
; Phase 0: Hand-written .spc stubs for p-code VM syscall wrappers

; _p24p_write_int ( n -- )
; Print signed integer to UART as decimal.
; Handles negative numbers (prints '-' prefix).
; Uses div/mod by 10 to extract digits, pushes onto eval stack
; in reverse order with a zero sentinel, then prints from most
; significant to least significant via sys 1 (PUTC).
.proc _p24p_write_int 1
    loada 0              ; load argument n
    ; check for negative
    dup                  ; n n
    push 0               ; n n 0
    lt                   ; n (n<0?)
    jz positive
    ; print minus sign
    push 45              ; n '-'
    sys 1                ; n
    neg                  ; -n
positive:
    storel 0             ; local0 = abs(n)
    push 0               ; push sentinel
extract:
    loadl 0              ; load n
    push 10
    mod                  ; n % 10
    push 48
    add                  ; n % 10 + '0' = digit char
    loadl 0
    push 10
    div                  ; n / 10
    storel 0             ; update n = n / 10
    loadl 0
    jnz extract          ; if n != 0, extract more digits
wi_print:
    dup
    jz wi_done
    sys 1                ; PUTC digit
    jmp wi_print
wi_done:
    drop                 ; discard sentinel
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_write_bool ( b -- )
; Print TRUE or FALSE to UART.
; Compares argument to 0: if zero prints FALSE, otherwise prints TRUE.
; Each character is pushed and output via sys 1 (PUTC).
.proc _p24p_write_bool 0
    loada 0              ; load argument b
    jz wb_false
    ; print "TRUE"
    push 84              ; 'T'
    sys 1
    push 82              ; 'R'
    sys 1
    push 85              ; 'U'
    sys 1
    push 69              ; 'E'
    sys 1
    jmp wb_done
wb_false:
    ; print "FALSE"
    push 70              ; 'F'
    sys 1
    push 65              ; 'A'
    sys 1
    push 76              ; 'L'
    sys 1

Now I understand the format. Let me create the merged test cases. The key difference from the e2e fixtures is that the linker strips `.module`/`.export`/`.extern`/`.endmodule` and puts `main` first. Let me create two test cases:

1. **merged_int.spc**: prints "42\n" using `_p24p_write_int` + `_p24p_write_ln`
2. **merged_str.spc**: prints "Hello\n" using `.data` + `_p24p_write_str`

Let me first check what `_p24p_write_str` looks like.

sys 1
    push 83              ; 'S'
    sys 1
    push 69              ; 'E'
    sys 1
wb_done:
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_write_ln ( -- )
; Print newline (LF) to UART via sys 1 (PUTC).
.proc _p24p_write_ln 0
    push 10              ; LF
    sys 1
    ret 0
.end

; _p24p_write_str ( addr -- )
; Print null-terminated string from data segment to UART.
; Walks bytes via loadb, calls sys 1 (PUTC) for each until null.
.proc _p24p_write_str 1
    loada 0              ; load string address
    storel 0             ; local0 = pointer
ws_loop:
    loadl 0              ; load pointer
    loadb                ; load byte at pointer
    dup
    jz ws_done           ; null terminator -> done
    sys 1                ; PUTC
    loadl 0
    push 1
    add
    storel 0             ; pointer++
    jmp ws_loop
ws_done:
    drop                 ; discard null byte
    ret 1
.end

; Phase 1: Standard functions
; Hand-written .spc until p24c supports function compilation.
; Will be replaced with Pascal-compiled versions when p24c Phase 1 lands.

; _p24p_abs ( x -- |x| )
; Absolute value. Returns x if x >= 0, else -x.
.proc _p24p_abs 0
    loada 0              ; load x
    dup
    push 0
    lt                   ; x < 0?
    jz abs_done
    neg                  ; negate if negative
abs_done:
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_odd ( x -- x mod 2 <> 0 )
; Returns true (1) if x is odd, false (0) if even.
.proc _p24p_odd 0
    loada 0              ; load x
    push 2
    mod                  ; x mod 2
    push 0
    ne                   ; result != 0 -> true
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_ord ( c -- c )
; Character to integer. Identity on this VM (chars are integers).
.proc _p24p_ord 0
    loada 0
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_chr ( n -- n )
; Integer to character. Identity on this VM (chars are integers).
.proc _p24p_chr 0
    loada 0
    ret 1
.end

content.text:
Good. Now I notice something important — the full runtime's `_p24p_write_int` does NOT use `enter`/`leave`, but the e2e fixture version DOES. The full runtime uses `loada 0; storel 0` without `enter`. This is a key difference. The runtime procedures in the full runtime.spc rely on `storel` writing to local slot 0 — but without `enter`, local slots might not be allocated properly.

Let me create a minimal merged_int.spc first — just main + write_int + write_ln:

file.content:
; read_all_input — read UART into input_buf until EOT (0x04)
; ============================================================
; Non-leaf. Clobbers: r0, r1, r2.
read_all_input:
    push r1
    ; Initialize write pointer and length
    la r0, input_buf
    la r2, rai_ptr
    sw r0, 0(r2)
    lc r0, 0
    la r2, input_len
    sw r0, 0(r2)

rai_loop:
    ; Poll UART RX ready
    la r2, -65280
rai_poll:
    lbu r0, 1(r2)
    push r2
    lc r2, 1
    and r0, r2
    pop r2
    ceq r0, z
    brt rai_poll
    ; Read byte
    lbu r0, 0(r2)
    ; Check for EOT (0x04)
    lc r2, 4
    ceq r0, r2
    brf rai_not_eot
    la r0, rai_done
    jmp (r0)
rai_not_eot:
    ; Store byte in buffer
    la r2, rai_ptr
    lw r2, 0(r2)
    sb r0, 0(r2)
    ; Advance pointer
    la r2, rai_ptr
    lw r0, 0(r2)
    add r0, 1
    sw r0, 0(r2)
    ; Increment length
    la r2, input_len
    lw r0, 0(r2)
    add r0, 1
    sw r0, 0(r2)
    bra rai_loop

rai_done:

content.text:
Interesting! The output shows:
- `PASM\n` (boot message)
- `CODE 64 0 1 42 0 0 51 16 0 0 51 85 0 0 0 65 64 1 72 0 3...` (assembly listing)
- `RUN\n`
- **`42\n`** (the actual program output!)
- `HALT\n`

The program actually **works** for merged_int.spc! It outputs "42\n" correctly. No TRAP 3! Let me test merged_str.spc:

input.command: grep "UART TX" <(cor24-run --run pvmasm.s -u "$(cat tests/merged_str.spc)$(printf '\x04')" --speed 0 -n 50000000 2>&1) | tail -40

content.text:
Both tests work! `merged_str.spc` outputs "Hello\n" and halts normally. The buffer fixes from earlier (name_pool 512→1024, input_buf 2KB→16KB) apparently resolved the TRAP 3 issue for these simpler linked programs.

The original TRAP 3 was with the FULL runtime (29 procedures). Let me create a more realistic test with more runtime procedures to reproduce the original failure.

211	; Phase 1: Runtime checks
212	; Hand-written .spc until p24c supports procedure compilation.
213	; Pascal source: src/checks.pas
214	
215	; _p24p_bounds_check ( index low high -- )
216	; Array bounds violation handler. Called by compiler-generated
217	; array access code. If index < low or index > high, prints
218	; "BOUNDS" to UART and halts.
219	.proc _p24p_bounds_check 0
220	    loada 2              ; load index (first pushed)
221	    loada 1              ; load low (second pushed)
222	    lt                   ; index < low?
223	    jnz bc_fail
224	    loada 2              ; load index
225	    loada 0              ; load high (last pushed)
226	    gt                   ; index > high?
227	    jnz bc_fail
228	    ret 3                ; in range, return normally
229	bc_fail:
230	    ; print "BOUNDS\n"
231	    push 66              ; 'B'
232	    sys 1
233	    push 79              ; 'O'
234	    sys 1
235	    push 85              ; 'U'
236	    sys 1
237	    push 78              ; 'N'
238	    sys 1
239	    push 68              ; 'D'
240	    sys 1
241	    push 83              ; 'S'
242	    sys 1
243	    push 10              ; LF
244	    sys 1
245	    sys 0                ; HALT
246	.end
247	
248	; _p24p_nil_check ( ptr -- )
249	; Nil pointer dereference handler. Called before pointer
250	; dereference. If ptr = 0, prints "NIL" and halts.
251	; Otherwise returns with ptr still on stack for use.
252	.proc _p24p_nil_check 0
253	    loada 0              ; load ptr
254	    jnz nc_ok            ; non-nil, safe
255	    ; print "NIL\n"
256	    push 78              ; 'N'
257	    sys 1
258	    push 73              ; 'I'
259	    sys 1
260	    push 76              ; 'L'
261	    sys 1
262	    push 10              ; LF
263	    sys 1
264	    sys 0                ; HALT
265	nc_ok:
266	    loada 0              ; return ptr for caller to use
267	    ret 1
268	.end
269	
270	; Phase 1: Read support
271	; Hand-written .spc until p24c supports function compilation.
272	; Pascal source: src/read.pas
273	
274	; _p24p_read_char ( -- c )
275	; Read single character from UART via sys 2 (GETC).
276	.proc _p24p_read_char 0
277	    sys 2                ; GETC -> push char
278	    ret 0
279	.end
280	
281	; _p24p_read_int ( -- n )
282	; Read signed integer from UART. Skips leading whitespace,
283	; handles optional +/- sign, accumulates decimal digits.
284	; local0 = n (accumulator), local1 = neg flag, local2 = current char
285	.proc _p24p_read_int 3
286	    push 0
287	    storel 0             ; n = 0
288	    push 0
289	    storel 1             ; neg = 0
290	    ; read first char, skip whitespace
291	    sys 2
292	    storel 2             ; c = getc
293	ri_ws:
294	    loadl 2
295	    push 32              ; space
296	    eq
297	    jnz ri_skip
298	    loadl 2
299	    push 9               ; tab
300	    eq
301	    jnz ri_skip
302	    jmp ri_sign
303	ri_skip:
304	    sys 2
305	    storel 2             ; c = getc
306	    jmp ri_ws
307	ri_sign:
308	    ; check for minus
309	    loadl 2
310	    push 45              ; '-'
311	    ne
312	    jnz ri_plus
313	    push 1
314	    storel 1             ; neg = 1
315	    sys 2
316	    storel 2             ; c = getc
317	    jmp ri_digits
318	ri_plus:
319	    ; check for plus
320	    loadl 2
321	    push 43              ; '+'
322	    ne
323	    jnz ri_digits
324	    sys 2
325	    storel 2             ; c = getc
326	ri_digits:
327	    ; check if c is a digit (48..57)
328	    loadl 2
329	    push 48
330	    lt
331	    jnz ri_negate        ; c < '0', stop
332	    loadl 2
333	    push 57
334	    gt
335	    jnz ri_negate        ; c > '9', stop
336	    ; n = n * 10 + (c - 48)
337	    loadl 0
338	    push 10
339	    mul
340	    loadl 2
341	    push 48
342	    sub
343	    add
344	    storel 0             ; n = n * 10 + digit
345	    sys 2
346	    storel 2             ; c = getc
347	    jmp ri_digits
348	ri_negate:
349	    loadl 1
350	    jz ri_done
351	    loadl 0
352	    neg
353	    storel 0             ; n = -n
354	ri_done:
355	    loadl 0              ; push result
356	    ret 0
357	.end

359	; _p24p_read_ln ( -- )
360	; Consume characters from UART until LF (10) is read.
361	.proc _p24p_read_ln 1
362	    sys 2
363	    storel 0             ; c = getc
364	rl_loop:
365	    loadl 0
366	    push 10              ; LF
367	    eq
368	    jnz rl_done
369	    sys 2
370	    storel 0             ; c = getc
371	    jmp rl_loop
372	rl_done:
373	    ret 0
374	.end
375	
376	; Phase 2: Heap management
377	; Hand-written .spc until p24c supports pointer/record compilation.
378	; Pascal source: src/heap.pas
379	
380	; Globals for heap tracking
381	; _h_ac = allocation count, _h_fc = free count
382	; _h_pt = pointer tracking table (16 slots)
383	.global _h_ac 1
384	.global _h_fc 1
385	.global _h_pt 16
386	
387	; _p24p_heap_init ( -- )
388	; Initialize heap tracking. Zero counters and pointer table.
389	.proc _p24p_heap_init 1
390	    push 0
391	    storeg _h_ac            ; alloc_count = 0
392	    push 0
393	    storeg _h_fc            ; free_count = 0
394	    ; zero tracking table
395	    push 0
396	    storel 0                ; i = 0
397	hi_loop:
398	    loadl 0
399	    push 16
400	    ge
401	    jnz hi_done             ; if i >= 16, done
402	    ; compute address of _h_pt[i] and store 0
403	    push 0
404	    addrg _h_pt             ; base address of table
405	    loadl 0                 ; i
406	    push 3
407	    mul                     ; i * 3 (byte offset)
408	    add                     ; base + i*3
409	    store                   ; mem[base + i*3] = 0
410	    loadl 0
411	    push 1
412	    add
413	    storel 0                ; i++
414	    jmp hi_loop
415	hi_done:
416	    ret 0
417	.end
418	
419	; _p24p_new ( size -- addr )
420	; Allocate size words via sys 4 (ALLOC). Track pointer.
421	.proc _p24p_new 2
422	    loada 0                 ; load size
423	    sys 4                   ; ALLOC -> addr on stack
424	    storel 0                ; local0 = addr
425	    ; alloc_count++
426	    loadg _h_ac
427	    push 1
428	    add
429	    storeg _h_ac
430	    ; find empty slot in tracking table
431	    push 0
432	    storel 1                ; i = 0
433	nw_loop:
434	    loadl 1
435	    push 16
436	    ge
437	    jnz nw_done             ; table full, skip tracking
438	    ; load _h_pt[i]
439	    addrg _h_pt
440	    loadl 1
441	    push 3
442	    mul
443	    add
444	    load                    ; _h_pt[i]
445	    jnz nw_next             ; slot occupied, try next
446	    ; store addr in empty slot
447	    loadl 0                 ; addr
448	    addrg _h_pt
449	    loadl 1
450	    push 3
451	    mul
452	    add
453	    store                   ; _h_pt[i] = addr
454	    jmp nw_done
455	nw_next:
456	    loadl 1
457	    push 1
458	    add
459	    storel 1                ; i++
460	    jmp nw_loop
461	nw_done:
462	    loadl 0                 ; push addr as return value
463	    ret 1
464	.end
465	
466	; _p24p_dispose ( ptr -- )
467	; Free heap memory via sys 5 (FREE). Remove from tracking.
468	.proc _p24p_dispose 1
469	    loada 0                 ; load ptr
470	    sys 5                   ; FREE
471	    ; free_count++
472	    loadg _h_fc
473	    push 1
474	    add
475	    storeg _h_fc
476	    ; find and remove ptr from tracking table
477	    push 0
478	    storel 0                ; i = 0
479	dp_loop:
480	    loadl 0
481	    push 16
482	    ge
483	    jnz dp_done             ; not found, done
484	    ; load _h_pt[i]
485	    addrg _h_pt
486	    loadl 0
487	    push 3
488	    mul
489	    add
490	    load                    ; _h_pt[i]
491	    loada 0                 ; ptr
492	    ne
493	    jnz dp_next             ; not this one
494	    ; clear the slot
495	    push 0
496	    addrg _h_pt
497	    loadl 0
498	    push 3
499	    mul
500	    add
501	    store                   ; _h_pt[i] = 0
502	    jmp dp_done
503	dp_next:
504	    loadl 0
505	    push 1
506	    add
507	    storel 0                ; i++
508	    jmp dp_loop
509	dp_done:
510	    ret 1
511	.end
512	
513	; _p24p_leak_report ( -- )
514	; Report unfreed allocations. Prints "LEAK:N" or "OK:0".
515	.proc _p24p_leak_report 1
516	    loadg _h_ac
517	    loadg _h_fc
518	    sub
519	    storel 0                ; leaks = alloc_count - free_count
520	    loadl 0
521	    push 0
522	    gt
523	    jz lr_ok
524	    ; print "LEAK:"
525	    push 76                 ; 'L'
526	    sys 1
527	    push 69                 ; 'E'
528	    sys 1
529	    push 65                 ; 'A'
530	    sys 1
531	    push 75                 ; 'K'
532	    sys 1
533	    push 58                 ; ':'
534	    sys 1
535	    loadl 0                 ; push leak count
536	    jmp lr_print
537	lr_ok:
538	    ; print "OK:"
539	    push 79                 ; 'O'
540	    sys 1
541	    push 75                 ; 'K'
542	    sys 1
543	    push 58                 ; ':'
544	    sys 1
545	    push 0                  ; push 0
546	lr_print:
547	    ; print the number using write_int logic inline

file.content:
loada 0
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_succ ( x -- x+1 )
; Next ordinal value.
.proc _p24p_succ 0
    loada 0
    push 1
    add
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_pred ( x -- x-1 )
; Previous ordinal value.
.proc _p24p_pred 0
    loada 0
    push 1
    sub
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_sqr ( x -- x*x )
; Square of integer.
.proc _p24p_sqr 0
    loada 0
    dup
    mul
    ret 1
.end

; Phase 1: Runtime checks
; Hand-written .spc until p24c supports procedure compilation.
; Pascal source: src/checks.pas

; _p24p_bounds_check ( index low high -- )
; Array bounds violation handler. Called by compiler-generated
; array access code. If index < low or index > high, prints
; "BOUNDS" to UART and halts.
.proc _p24p_bounds_check 0
    loada 2              ; load index (first pushed)
    loada 1              ; load low (second pushed)
    lt                   ; index < low?
    jnz bc_fail
    loada 2              ; load index
    loada 0              ; load high (last pushed)
    gt                   ; index > high?
    jnz bc_fail
    ret 3                ; in range, return normally
bc_fail:
    ; print "BOUNDS\n"
    push 66              ; 'B'
    sys 1
    push 79              ; 'O'
    sys 1
    push 85              ; 'U'
    sys 1
    push 78              ; 'N'
    sys 1
    push 68              ; 'D'
    sys 1
    push 83              ; 'S'
    sys 1
    push 10              ; LF
    sys 1
    sys 0                ; HALT
.end

; _p24p_nil_check ( ptr -- )
; Nil pointer dereference handler. Called before pointer
; dereference. If ptr = 0, prints "NIL" and halts.
; Otherwise returns with ptr still on stack for use.
.proc _p24p_nil_check 0
    loada 0              ; load ptr
    jnz nc_ok            ; non-nil, safe
    ; print "NIL\n"
    push 78              ; 'N'
    sys 1
    push 73              ; 'I'
    sys 1
    push 76              ; 'L'
    sys 1
    push 10              ; LF
    sys 1
    sys 0                ; HALT
nc_ok:
    loada 0              ; return ptr for caller to use
    ret 1
.end

; Phase 1: Read support
; Hand-written .spc until p24c supports function compilation.
; Pascal source: src/read.pas

; _p24p_read_char ( -- c )
; Read single character from UART via sys 2 (GETC).
.proc _p24p_read_char 0
    sys 2                ; GETC -> push char
    ret 0
.end

; _p24p_read_int ( -- n )
; Read signed integer from UART. Skips leading whitespace,
; handles optional +/- sign, accumulates decimal digits.
; local0 = n (accumulator), local1 = neg flag, local2 = current char
.proc _p24p_read_int 3
    push 0
    storel 0             ; n = 0
    push 0
    storel 1             ; neg = 0
    ; read first char, skip whitespace
    sys 2
    storel 2             ; c = getc
ri_ws:
    loadl 2
    push 32              ; space
    eq
    jnz ri_skip
    loadl 2
    push 9               ; tab
    eq
    jnz ri_skip
    jmp ri_sign
ri_skip:
    sys 2
    storel 2             ; c = getc
    jmp ri_ws
ri_sign:
    ; check for minus
    loadl 2
    push 45              ; '-'
    ne
    jnz ri_plus
    push 1
    storel 1             ; neg = 1
    sys 2
    storel 2             ; c = getc
    jmp ri_digits
ri_plus:
    ; check for plus
    loadl 2
    push 43              ; '+'
    ne
    jnz ri_digits
    sys 2
    storel 2             ; c = getc
ri_digits:
    ; check if c is a digit (48..57)
    loadl 2
    push 48
    lt
    jnz ri_negate        ; c < '0', stop
    loadl 2
    push 57
    gt
    jnz ri_negate        ; c > '9', stop
    ; n = n * 10 + (c - 48)
    loadl 0
    push 10
    mul
    loadl 2
    push 48
    sub
    add
    storel 0             ; n = n * 10 + digit
    sys 2
    storel 2             ; c = getc
    jmp ri_digits
ri_negate:
    loadl 1
    jz ri_done
    loadl 0
    neg
    storel 0             ; n = -n
ri_done:
    loadl 0              ; push result
    ret 0
.end

; _p24p_read_ln ( -- )
; Consume characters from UART until LF (10) is read.
.proc _p24p_read_ln 1
    sys 2
    storel 0             ; c = getc
rl_loop:
    loadl 0
    push 10              ; LF
    eq
    jnz rl_done
    sys 2
    storel 0             ; c = getc
    jmp rl_loop
rl_done:
    ret 0
.end

; Phase 2: Heap management
; Hand-written .spc until p24c supports pointer/record compilation.
; Pascal source: src/heap.pas

; Globals for heap tracking
; _h_ac = allocation count, _h_fc = free count
; _h_pt = pointer tracking table (16 slots)
.global _h_ac 1
.global _h_fc 1
.global _h_pt 16

; _p24p_heap_init ( -- )
; Initialize heap tracking. Zero counters and pointer table.
.proc _p24p_heap_init 1
    push 0
    storeg _h_ac            ; alloc_count = 0
    push 0
    storeg _h_fc            ; free_count = 0
    ; zero tracking table
    push 0
    storel 0                ; i = 0
hi_loop:
    loadl 0
    push 16
    ge
    jnz hi_done             ; if i >= 16, done
    ; compute address of _h_pt[i] and store 0
    push 0
    addrg _h_pt             ; base address of table
    loadl 0                 ; i
    push 3
    mul                     ; i * 3 (byte offset)
    add                     ; base + i*3
    store                   ; mem[base + i*3] = 0
    loadl 0
    push 1
    add
    storel 0                ; i++
    jmp hi_loop
hi_done:
    ret 0
.end

; _p24p_new ( size -- addr )
; Allocate size words via sys 4 (ALLOC). Track pointer.
.proc _p24p_new 2
    loada 0                 ; load size
    sys 4                   ; ALLOC -> addr on stack
    storel 0                ; local0 = addr
    ; alloc_count++
    loadg _h_ac
    push 1
    add
    storeg _h_ac
    ; find empty slot in tracking table
    push 0
    storel 1                ; i = 0
nw_loop:
    loadl 1
    push 16
    ge
    jnz nw_done             ; table full, skip tracking
    ; load _h_pt[i]
    addrg _h_pt
    loadl 1
    push 3
    mul
    add
    load                    ; _h_pt[i]
    jnz nw_next             ; slot occupied, try next
    ; store addr in empty slot
    loadl 0                 ; addr
    addrg _h_pt
    loadl 1
    push 3
    mul
    add
    store                   ; _h_pt[i] = addr
    jmp nw_done
nw_next:
    loadl 1
    push 1
    add
    storel 1                ; i++
    jmp nw_loop
nw_done:
    loadl 0                 ; push addr as return value
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_dispose ( ptr -- )
; Free heap memory via sys 5 (FREE). Remove from tracking.
.proc _p24p_dispose 1
    loada 0                 ; load ptr
    sys 5                   ; FREE
    ; free_count++
    loadg _h_fc
    push 1
    add
    storeg _h_fc
    ; find and remove ptr from tracking table
    push 0
    storel 0                ; i = 0
dp_loop:
    loadl 0
    push 16
    ge
    jnz dp_done             ; not found, done
    ; load _h_pt[i]
    addrg _h_pt
    loadl 0
    push 3
    mul
    add
    load                    ; _h_pt[i]
    loada 0                 ; ptr
    ne
    jnz dp_next             ; not this one
    ; clear the slot
    push 0
    addrg _h_pt
    loadl 0
    push 3
    mul
    add
    store                   ; _h_pt[i] = 0
    jmp dp_done
dp_next:
    loadl 0
    push 1
    add
    storel 0                ; i++
    jmp dp_loop
dp_done:
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_leak_report ( -- )
; Report unfreed allocations. Prints "LEAK:N" or "OK:0".
.proc _p24p_leak_report 1
    loadg _h_ac
    loadg _h_fc
    sub
    storel 0                ; leaks = alloc_count - free_count
    loadl 0
    push 0
    gt
    jz lr_ok
    ; print "LEAK:"
    push 76                 ; 'L'
    sys 1
    push 69                 ; 'E'
    sys 1
    push 65                 ; 'A'
    sys 1
    push 75                 ; 'K'
    sys 1
    push 58                 ; ':'
    sys 1
    loadl 0                 ; push leak count
    jmp lr_print
lr_ok:
    ; print "OK:"
    push 79                 ; 'O'
    sys 1
    push 75                 ; 'K'
    sys 1
    push 58                 ; ':'
    sys 1
    push 0                  ; push 0
lr_print:
    ; print the number using write_int logic inline
    ; (can't call _p24p_write_int from here since it uses ret 1)
    dup
    push 0
    lt
    jz lr_pos
    push 45
    sys 1
    neg
lr_pos:
    storel 0
    push 0                  ; sentinel
lr_ext:
    loadl 0
    push 10
    mod
    push 48
    add                     ; digit char
    loadl 0
    push 10
    div
    storel 0
    loadl 0
    jnz lr_ext
lr_prt:
    dup
    jz lr_end
    sys 1
    jmp lr_prt
lr_end:
    drop                    ; discard sentinel
    push 10                 ; LF
    sys 1

content.text:
It's stuck in `rai_poll` (address 0x000128) — the UART RX read loop. The input was truncated because it exceeded the buffer, so the EOT (0x04) byte was never stored. The program spins forever waiting for more input.

Wait — actually the issue is different. The `-u` flag sends the full input to the emulator's UART, but the COR24 `read_all_input` reads byte-by-byte from the UART into a 16KB buffer. If the input is >16KB, the buffer overflows and corrupts memory. Let me check if it's stuck reading or if it has overflowed.

ret 0
.end

; Phase 2: Write formatting
; Hand-written .spc until p24c supports procedure compilation.
; Pascal source: src/write_fmt.pas

; _p24p_write_char ( c -- )
; Write single character to UART via sys 1 (PUTC).
.proc _p24p_write_char 0
    loada 0              ; load c
    sys 1                ; PUTC
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_write_int_w ( n width -- )
; Write integer n right-justified in field of given width.
; Calling convention: push n, push width, call. loada 0=width, loada 1=n.
; Counts digits+sign, pads with spaces, then prints number.
.proc _p24p_write_int_w 3
    ; local0 = tmp, local1 = digits, local2 = neg
    push 0
    storel 2                ; neg = 0
    loada 1                 ; load n
    dup
    push 0
    lt
    jz iw_pos
    push 1
    storel 2                ; neg = 1
    neg                     ; tmp = -n
    jmp iw_count
iw_pos:
    ; tmp = n (already on stack)
iw_count:
    storel 0                ; local0 = abs(n)
    push 0
    storel 1                ; digits = 0
iw_dloop:
    loadl 1
    push 1
    add
    storel 1                ; digits++
    loadl 0
    push 10
    div
    storel 0                ; tmp = tmp / 10
    loadl 0
    jnz iw_dloop            ; while tmp != 0
    ; digits += neg
    loadl 1
    loadl 2
    add
    storel 1                ; digits = digits + neg
    ; pad with spaces: while digits < width
iw_pad:
    loadl 1
    loada 0                 ; width
    ge
    jnz iw_num              ; if digits >= width, done padding
    push 32                 ; space
    sys 1
    loadl 1
    push 1
    add
    storel 1                ; digits++ (count padding)
    jmp iw_pad
iw_num:
    ; now print the number using write_int logic inline
    loada 1                 ; load n
    dup
    push 0
    lt
    jz iw_npos
    push 45                 ; '-'
    sys 1
    neg
iw_npos:
    storel 0                ; local0 = abs(n)
    push 0                  ; sentinel
iw_ext:
    loadl 0
    push 10
    mod
    push 48
    add                     ; digit char
    loadl 0
    push 10
    div
    storel 0
    loadl 0
    jnz iw_ext
iw_prt:
    dup
    jz iw_end
    sys 1
    jmp iw_prt
iw_end:
    drop                    ; discard sentinel
    ret 2
.end

; _p24p_write_char_w ( c width -- )
; Write character c right-justified in field of given width.
; Calling convention: push c, push width, call. loada 0=width, loada 1=c.
.proc _p24p_write_char_w 1
    loada 0                 ; load width
    storel 0                ; local0 = remaining
cw_pad:
    loadl 0
    push 1
    le
    jnz cw_emit             ; if remaining <= 1, done padding
    push 32                 ; space
    sys 1
    loadl 0
    push 1
    sub
    storel 0                ; remaining--
    jmp cw_pad
cw_emit:
    loada 1                 ; load c
    sys 1                   ; PUTC
    ret 2
.end

; _p24p_write_bool_w ( b width -- )
; Write boolean right-justified in field of given width.
; TRUE=4 chars, FALSE=5 chars.
; Calling convention: push b, push width, call. loada 0=width, loada 1=b.
.proc _p24p_write_bool_w 2
    ; local0 = len, local1 = remaining width
    loada 1                 ; load b
    jz bw_false_len
    push 4
    jmp bw_have_len
bw_false_len:
    push 5
bw_have_len:
    storel 0                ; len
    loada 0                 ; width
    storel 1
bw_pad:
    loadl 0
    loadl 1
    ge
    jnz bw_emit             ; if len >= width, done
    push 32
    sys 1
    loadl 1
    push 1
    sub
    storel 1                ; width--
    jmp bw_pad
bw_emit:
    loada 1                 ; load b
    call _p24p_write_bool
    ret 2
.end

; _p24p_write_str_w ( addr width -- )
; Write string right-justified in field of given width.
; First counts string length, then pads and prints.
; Calling convention: push addr, push width, call. loada 0=width, loada 1=addr.
.proc _p24p_write_str_w 2
    ; local0 = len, local1 = ptr
    push 0
    storel 0                ; len = 0
    loada 1                 ; load addr
    storel 1                ; ptr = addr
sw_count:
    loadl 1
    loadb                   ; byte at ptr
    jz sw_pad_start         ; null terminator -> done counting
    loadl 0
    push 1
    add
    storel 0                ; len++
    loadl 1
    push 1
    add
    storel 1                ; ptr++
    jmp sw_count
sw_pad_start:
    loadl 0
    loada 0                 ; width
    ge
    jnz sw_emit             ; if len >= width, no padding
    push 32
    sys 1
    loada 0
    push 1
    sub
    storea 0                ; width-- (modify arg for loop)
    jmp sw_pad_start
sw_emit:
    loada 1                 ; addr
    call _p24p_write_str
    ret 2
.end

; Phase 2: I/O state functions
; Hand-written .spc until p24c supports function compilation.
; Pascal source: src/io_state.pas

; Globals for I/O lookahead buffer
; _io_la = lookahead char (-1 = empty)
; _io_ef = eof flag (0 = not eof, 1 = eof)
.global _io_la 1
.global _io_ef 1

; _p24p_eof ( -- flag )
; Returns 1 if at end of input (EOT byte 0x04), 0 otherwise.
; Uses a one-character lookahead buffer.
.proc _p24p_eof 0
    loadg _io_la
    push 0
    lt
    jz ef_have              ; if lookahead >= 0, we have a buffered char
    ; buffer empty, read a char
    sys 2                   ; GETC
    storeg _io_la           ; store in lookahead
ef_have:
    loadg _io_la
    push 4                  ; EOT
    eq                      ; lookahead == EOT?
    ret 0
.end

; _p24p_eoln ( -- flag )
; Returns 1 if at end of line (LF byte 10) or at EOF.
; Uses same lookahead buffer as eof.
.proc _p24p_eoln 0
    loadg _io_la
    push 0
    lt
    jz el_have
    sys 2                   ; GETC
    storeg _io_la
el_have:
    loadg _io_la
    push 10                 ; LF
    eq
    jnz el_true
    loadg _io_la
    push 4                  ; EOT
    eq
    jnz el_true
    push 0                  ; not at eoln
    ret 0
el_true:
    push 1
    ret 0
.end

; _p24p_subrange_check ( value low high -- )
; Subrange violation handler. Like bounds_check but prints "RANGE".
.proc _p24p_subrange_check 0
    loada 2              ; load value (first pushed)
    loada 1              ; load low
    lt                   ; value < low?
    jnz sr_fail
    loada 2              ; load value
    loada 0              ; load high
    gt                   ; value > high?
    jnz sr_fail
    ret 3                ; in range, return normally
sr_fail:
    push 82              ; 'R'
    sys 1
    push 65              ; 'A'
    sys 1
    push 78              ; 'N'
    sys 1
    push 71              ; 'G'
    sys 1
    push 69              ; 'E'
    sys 1
    push 10              ; LF
    sys 1
    sys 0                ; HALT
.end

; Hardware unit routines
; These support `uses Hardware` in Pascal programs.

; _p24p_set_led ( n -- )
; Write LED state via sys 3 (LED).
.proc _p24p_set_led 0
    loada 0              ; load LED state
    sys 3                ; LED
    ret 1
.end

; _p24p_read_switch ( -- n )
; Read switch state. Stub returning 0 (no VM syscall yet).
.proc _p24p_read_switch 0
    push 0               ; stub: always 0
    ret 0
.end

; _p24p_halt ( -- )
; Stop execution via sys 0 (HALT).
.proc _p24p_halt 0
    sys 0                ; HALT
.end

.endmodule

content.text: 93 symbols, 64 max → overflow. Now I need a mid-sized version that fits but still exercises the runtime. Let me create one with just the procedures the app actually calls, plus a few more to stress the symbol table:

input.command:
# Create a medium test: main + 8 runtime procs, targeting ~50-60 symbols
# Include write_int, write_bool, write_ln, write_str, abs, odd, bounds_check, nil_check
cat > tests/merged_mid_int.spc << 'SPCEOF'
; merged_mid_int.spc — Medium linked program (8 runtime procs)
; Tests main calling write_int/write_ln, with several unused runtime procs
; Expected output: 42\nTRUE\n-7\n

; --- module: app ---
.proc main 0
    push 42
    call _p24p_write_int
    call _p24p_write_ln
    push 1
    call _p24p_write_bool
    call _p24p_write_ln
    push -7
    call _p24p_write_int
    call _p24p_write_ln
    halt
.end

; --- module: runtime ---

.proc _p24p_write_int 1
    loada 0
    dup
    push 0
    lt
    jz positive
    push 45
    sys 1
    neg
positive:
    storel 0
    push 0
extract:
    loadl 0
    push 10
    mod
    push 48
    add
    loadl 0
    push 10
    div
    storel 0
    loadl 0
    jnz extract
wi_print:
    dup
    jz wi_done
    sys 1
    jmp wi_print
wi_done:
    drop
    ret 1
.end

.proc _p24p_write_bool 0
    loada 0
    jz wb_false
    push 84
    sys 1
    push 82
    sys 1
    push 85
    sys 1
    push 69
    sys 1
    jmp wb_done
wb_false:
    push 70
    sys 1
    push 65
    sys 1
    push 76
    sys 1
    push 83
    sys 1
    push 69
    sys 1
wb_done:
    ret 1
.end

.proc _p24p_write_ln 0
    push 10
    sys 1
    ret 0
.end

.proc _p24p_write_str 1
    loada 0
    storel 0
ws_loop:
    loadl 0
    loadb
    dup
    jz ws_done
    sys 1
    loadl 0
    push 1
    add
    storel 0
    jmp ws_loop
ws_done:
    drop
    ret 1
.end

.proc _p24p_abs 0
    loada 0
    dup
    push 0
    lt
    jz abs_done
    neg
abs_done:
    ret 1
.end

.proc _p24p_odd 0
    loada 0
    push 2
    mod
    push 0
    ne
    ret 1
.end

.proc _p24p_bounds_check 0
    loada 2
    loada 1
    lt
    jnz bc_fail
    loada 2
    loada 0
    gt
    jnz bc_fail
    ret 3
bc_fail:
    push 66
    sys 1
    push 79
    sys 1
    push 85
    sys 1
    push 78
    sys 1
    push 68
    sys 1
    push 83
    sys 1
    push 10
    sys 1
    sys 0
.end

.proc _p24p_nil_check 0
    loada 0
    jnz nc_ok
    push 78
    sys 1
    push 73
    sys 1
    push 76
    sys 1
    push 10
    sys 1
    sys 0
nc_ok:
    loada 0
    ret 1
.end
SPCEOF
grep -cE '^\.(proc|global|data|const)|^[a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z_0-9]*:' tests/merged_mid_int.spc
grep "UART TX" <(cor24-run --run pvmasm.s -u "$(cat tests/merged_mid_int.spc)$(printf '\x04')" --speed 0 -n 50000000 2>&1) | tail -20

content.text:
Mid-sized works perfectly: outputs `42
TRUE
-7
HALT
`. No TRAP 3 with only 20 symbols.

Now I need to reproduce the TRAP 3. Let me create a test that pushes the symbol table closer to the limit. The original had 58 symbols per the observations. Let me create a test with ~58 symbols:

input.command:
# Create a test with lots of procedures - push toward the 64 symbol limit
# The original merged_int.spc had 21 procedures (all of pr24p runtime Phase 0+1)
# Let me include up to _p24p_read_ln (21 procs total) and count symbols
cat > /tmp/build_merged21.py << 'PYEOF'
# Build merged file with first 21 runtime procs (through Phase 1)
import re

with open('/Users/mike/github/softwarewrighter/pr24p/src/runtime.spc') as f:
    lines = f.readlines()

# Strip module directives
skip_dirs = {'.module', '.export', '.extern', '.endmodule'}
clean = []
proc_count = 0
for line in lines:
    stripped = line.strip()
    if any(stripped.startswith(d) for d in skip_dirs):
        continue
    if stripped.startswith('.proc'):
        proc_count += 1
    # Stop after 21st .end
    clean.append(line)
    if stripped == '.end' and proc_count >= 21:
        break

content.text:
Now I see the key mechanism (lines 200-216): UART input is fed **one byte per batch** (10000 instructions at speed 0), and only when `RX ready` is clear. This means the input drip-feeds at ~1 char per batch.

The issue with the 12.7KB file is: it takes many batches to feed all the input. With 100M instructions at batch_size=10000, that's 10000 batches, so max 10000 chars of UART input. 12.7KB = 12745 bytes — this exceeds 10000 chars!

Let me verify:

input.command:
# With speed=0, batch=10000, n=100000000: 100M/10000 = 10000 batches = max 10000 chars
# The 21-proc file is 12745 bytes + 1 byte EOT = 12746 bytes. Needs at least 12746 batches = 127,460,000 instructions
# Let me try with n=200000000
cor24-run --run pvmasm.s -u "$(cat tests/merged_21procs.spc)$(printf '\x04')" --speed 0 -n 200000000 2>&1 | grep "UART TX" | sed 's/.*] //' | tr -d "' " | sed "s/(.*//" | tr -d '\n'
input.description: Run with more instructions to feed all input

content.text:
With 200M instructions it works! Output: `PASM
 CODE...
 RUN
 42
 HALT
`. No TRAP 3.

So the issue is simply that with speed=0, the UART input feed rate is limited to ~1 char per 10000 instructions. The program needs `n_chars * 10000` instructions just to read all input.

Now let me test with the **full 32-proc runtime** (93 symbols) with even more instructions to see the actual SYM? FULL error:

input.command:
# Full runtime needs 19616 bytes * ~10000 instructions/byte = 196M just for input.
# Plus assembly time. Let's use 500M and check the output
cor24-run --run pvmasm.s -u "$(cat tests/merged_full_int.spc)$(printf '\x04')" --speed 0 -n 500000000 2>&1 | grep "UART TX" | sed 's/.*] //' | tr -d "' " | sed "s/(.*//" | tr -d '\n' | head -c 200

Original request: pvm.s init hardcodes `code = code_seg` (la r0, code_seg; sw r0, 18(fp)). When loading external .p24 binaries at a different address (e.g., 0x010000), the init overwrites our patched code_base. Fix: instead of `la r0, code_seg`, do `lw r0, code_ptr` where `code_ptr` is a word in the data section initialized to code_seg. Then `cor24-run --patch <code_ptr_addr>=0x010000` can override the code_base before pvm.s runs, and init will load the patched value. This is a 2-line change in pvm.s init (replace `la r0, code_seg` with `la r0, code_ptr; lw r0, 0(r0)`) plus adding a `code_ptr: .word code_seg` data entry. |

### REQ-018: p24p add UART-input mode for CLI compilation

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| From | p24p (self) |
| To | p24p |
| Status | **DONE** |
| Completed | 2026-03-27 |
| Priority | High |

| Detail | **DONE (commit 1b3ddf8).** src/main.c reads Pascal source via getchar until EOT, compiles to .spc on UART. Validated with countdown.pas. Original plan: compile arbitrary .pas files from CLI, p24p needs a UART-input mode: read Pascal source from UART until EOT (0x04), buffer it, then pass to parser_init(). This enables: `printf '%s\x04' "$(cat hello.pas)" \| cor24-run --run p24p.s --terminal --speed 0 --time 30` which outputs .spc on UART. Same pattern pvmasm.s uses for reading .spc input. Implementation: create src/main.c with UART read loop (getchar until EOT or EOF), call parser_init/parse_program, .spc output already goes to UART via printf. Compile with: `tc24r src/main.c -o p24p.s -I tc24r/include -I src`. |
| Blocked by | --- |
| Notes | This completes the CLI pipeline: `cat hello.pas \| p24p.s -> .spc \| pl24r -> merged.spc -> pa24r -> .p24 -> cor24-run + pvm.s`. All downstream tools (pl24r, pa24r, pvm.s, cor24-run) are working and verified. |
| **web-p24c interest** | This is the key enabler for in-browser Pascal compilation. web-p24c can pre-compile p24p to COR24 binary at build time (tc24r → p24p.s → assemble), then run it in a second EmulatorCore instance at runtime. User types Pascal → feed to p24p via UART → collect .spc → pl24r link → pa24r assemble → pvm.s execute. Same emulator-via-UART pattern we already use for pvm.s. Eager to integrate once UART-input mode lands. |

We need a standalone compiler binary that reads Pascal source from UART input and emits .spc to UART output. This enables CLI compilation: `cat hello.pas \| cor24-run --run p24p.s --terminal`. The binary should: (1) read Pascal source from UART until EOF/EOT, (2) compile to .spc, (3) emit .spc to UART TX, (4) halt. This is the missing piece for `hello.pas → .spc` at the command line. ### REQ-019: cor24-run fast CLI path: pre-assembled VM + .p24 loading

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| From | web-dv24r (coordinator) |
| To | cor24-rs |
| Status | **DONE** |
| Priority | High |
| Requested | 2026-03-27 |
| Completed | 2026-03-27 |
| Detail | The CLI path for running p-code programs is too slow because cor24-run re-assembles pvm.s every time (~2800 bytes, instant but unnecessary) and pvmasm.s is unusable for large runtimes (500M+ COR24 instructions to assemble 984 bytes of p-code). We need: (1) `--load-binary` should strip .p24 headers (first 18 bytes) and only load the body, OR accept raw binaries. (2) A `--patch addr=value` flag to write 24-bit words to memory after loading (needed to set vm_state.code_base to the .p24 load address). (3) Pre-assembled pvm.s binary support: `cor24-run --load-binary pvm.bin@0x000000 --load-binary hello.p24@0x010000 --entry 0x000000 --patch 0x09D7=0x010000 --terminal`. (4) Alternatively, a `--run-binary file.bin` flag that loads a pre-assembled COR24 binary instead of assembling .s source. The goal: `hello.pas -> p24p -> .spc -> pl24r -> pa24r -> .p24 -> cor24-run` with instant startup, no assembly at runtime. |
| Blocked by | — |


### REQ-020: pvm.s support external code_base via memory patch

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| From | web-dv24r (coordinator) |
| To | pv24a |
| Status | **DONE** |
| Priority | High |
| Requested | 2026-03-27 |
| Completed | 2026-03-27 |
| Detail | pvm.s init now uses `la r0, code_ptr; lw r0, 0(r0)` instead of `la r0, code_seg`. `code_ptr` is a `.word code_seg` — defaults to built-in code, patchable for external .p24. Original request: pvm.s init hardcodes `code = code_seg` (la r0, code_seg; sw r0, 18(fp)). When loading external .p24 binaries at a different address (e.g., 0x010000), the init overwrites our patched code_base. Fix: instead of `la r0, code_seg`, do `lw r0, code_ptr` where `code_ptr` is a word in the data section initialized to code_seg. Then `cor24-run --patch <code_ptr_addr>=0x010000` can override the code_base before pvm.s runs, and init will load the patched value. This is a 2-line change in pvm.s init (replace `la r0, code_seg` with `la r0, code_ptr; lw r0, 0(r0)`) plus adding a `code_ptr: .word code_seg` data entry. |
| Blocked by | — |

**LED support status:**
- **REQ-026** (REQUESTED): pvm.s sys 3 (LED) writes to wrong address — `la r2, -65024` (0xFF0200) should be `la r2, -65536` (0xFF0000). This is a pv24a bug I need to fix.
- **REQ-025** (appears twice — one is the p24p halt issue, the other): pv24a needs `sys 6` (read switch state) in pvm.s.
- **REQ-024** (DONE): pr24p Hardware unit with SetLED/ReadSwitch routines created.
- **REQ-023** (DONE): p24p `uses` clause implemented.
- **REQ-027** (ACKNOWLEDGED): web-p24c needs rebuild to pick up uses/Hardware support.

content.text:
OK so REQ-025 (sys 6) is just a stub header with no details yet. REQ-026 (LED address bug) is fully described.

Here's the answer for you:

**LED support is NOT complete yet.** Two pv24a bugs need fixing:

1. **REQ-026** (REQUESTED): `sys 3` (LED) writes to wrong address — `0xFF0200` instead of `0xFF0000`. One-line fix: change `la r2, -65024` to `la r2, -65536` in `pvm.s:1722`.

2. **REQ-025** (REQUESTED, stub): `sys 6` (read switch) needs to be added to pvm.s. Currently `_p24p_read_switch` in the runtime returns a hardcoded 0. pvm.s needs a new syscall handler that reads from the COR24 switch register.

**Wiki status:** REQ-025 is just a header (no detail body yet) — you mentioned the wiki update is coming "soon". REQ-026 is fully documented.

**Upstream readiness:**
- REQ-023 (p24p `uses` clause) — DONE
- REQ-024 (pr24p Hardware/System units) — DONE
- REQ-027 (web-p24c rebuild) — ACKNOWLEDGED, not started

**Can demos be built/tested now?** Not yet for Hardware features. The two pvm.s fixes (REQ-026 LED address + REQ-025 sys 6) need to land first. After that, web-p24c needs a rebuild (REQ-027) to pick up the `uses Hardware` support. The LED fix is trivial (~1 min); sys 6 is small (~20 lines of COR24 assembly). Both could be done this session.

Want me to pause the TRAP 3 investigation and fix REQ-026 + REQ-025 now?

file.content:
brt sys_getc
    ; Reload sys id
    push fp
    la r0, sys_id_temp
    push r0
    pop fp
    lw r0, 0(fp)
    pop fp
    ; id == 3 (LED)?
    lc r2, 3
    ceq r0, r2
    brt sys_led
    ; Reload sys id
    push fp
    la r0, sys_id_temp
    push r0
    pop fp
    lw r0, 0(fp)
    pop fp
    ; id == 4 (ALLOC)?
    lc r2, 4
    ceq r0, r2
    brt sys_alloc_j
    ; id == 5 (FREE)?
    lc r2, 5
    ceq r0, r2
    brt sys_free_j
    ; Unknown sys id — trap
    la r0, op_invalid
    jmp (r0)

; Jump trampolines for far handlers
sys_alloc_j:
    la r0, sys_alloc
    jmp (r0)
sys_free_j:
    la r0, sys_free
    jmp (r0)

; sys HALT (id=0): stop VM execution
sys_halt:
    ; fp = &vm_state
    lc r0, 1
    sw r0, 21(fp)
    la r0, vm_loop
    jmp (r0)

; sys PUTC (id=1): pop char from eval stack, send to UART
sys_putc:
    ; fp = &vm_state
    lw r2, 3(fp)
    add r2, -3
    sw r2, 3(fp)
    ; r2 = new esp = &TOS
    lw r0, 0(r2)
    ; r0 = char value
    push r0
    la r2, -65280
sys_putc_wait:
    lb r0, 1(r2)
    cls r0, z
    brt sys_putc_wait
    pop r0
    sb r0, 0(r2)
    la r0, vm_loop
    jmp (r0)

; sys GETC (id=2): read byte from UART, push onto eval stack
sys_getc:
    ; Poll UART status until RX ready (bit 0)
sys_getc_wait:
    la r2, -65280
    lbu r0, 1(r2)
    ; bit 0 = RX ready; mask it
    lc r2, 1
    and r0, r2
    ceq r0, z
    brt sys_getc_wait
    ; RX ready — read data byte
    la r2, -65280
    lbu r0, 0(r2)
    ; r0 = received byte; push onto eval stack
    la r2, vm_state
    push r2
    pop fp
    lw r2, 3(fp)
    ; r2 = esp
    sw r0, 0(r2)
    add r2, 3
    sw r2, 3(fp)
    la r0, vm_loop
    jmp (r0)

; sys LED (id=3): pop state from eval stack, write to LED port
sys_led:
    ; fp = &vm_state
    lw r2, 3(fp)
    add r2, -3
    sw r2, 3(fp)
    ; r2 = new esp = &TOS
    lw r0, 0(r2)
    ; r0 = LED state
    la r2, -65024
    sb r0, 0(r2)
    la r0, vm_loop
    jmp (r0)

; sys ALLOC (id=4): pop size, bump-allocate, push pointer
; Uses nonlocal_temps as scratch: [0]=size, [3]=old hp (return ptr)
sys_alloc:
    la r0, vm_state
    push r0
    pop fp
    ; Pop size from eval stack
    lw r2, 3(fp)
    add r2, -3
    sw r2, 3(fp)
    lw r0, 0(r2)
    ; r0 = size
    ; Save size to nonlocal_temps[0]
